prompt: A ScholarOps Offline Learning & Content Intake system with main features including multi-role access control, multi-source crawling with rule management, structured parsing and content standardization, assignments/quizzes with auto-grading workflow, visual timetable editing, and listing search with intelligent filtering. On the user side, the Vue.js web interface provides separate workspaces for Administrator, Content Curator, Instructor, Teaching Assistant, and Student roles, with navigation and actions dynamically enabled based on permission. Administrators manage users, assign roles, and review permission-change audit history. Curators configure offline crawl jobs by selecting a source profile, testing extraction on a sample page, and previewing standardized output before publishing; they can hot-update rules and revert to a prior version if a source changes. Instructors build question banks with difficulty levels (1–5) and knowledge tags, assemble quizzes from rules (for example, 20 questions with at least 5 medium and 5 hard), schedule timed release windows, and set submission limits such as one attempt with a 60-minute timer. Students see a combined dashboard: a searchable catalog of imported learning items with keyword filters, price range (when applicable for paid materials tracked internally), availability dates, and sorting by newest or popularity; a weekly/daily timetable editor that supports drag-and-drop rescheduling, merge/split sessions, and explicit “locked” periods that cannot be auto-shifted; and an assessment view with clear countdown timers, autosave every 15 seconds, and immediate feedback for objective questions plus wrong-answer review linked to instructor-provided explanations.On the system side, the backend uses Spring Boot to expose decoupled REST-style APIs consumed by the Vue.js client over the local network or localhost, keeping the entire deployment offline-runnable without any third-party identity, maps, payments, email, or push services. MySQL persists users, roles, permissions (enforced at both menu and API levels), audit logs, crawl rule versions, crawl runs, standardized content records, media metadata, schedules, question banks, quiz papers, submissions, grading states, and wrong-answer history. The crawler executes locally with per-source rate limits (default 30 requests/minute) and optional stored credentials for sources, protected via AES-256 encryption at rest; passwords are hashed with BCrypt and must be at least 12 characters with at least one upper-case letter, one lower-case letter, one number, and one symbol, and reset is handled via on-device admin-assisted reset requiring the old password or an administrator override logged with timestamp and workstation ID. Parsing supports XPath/CSS selectors, regex, and JSONPath with field mapping and type validation; the system standardizes timestamps to local time with explicit zone selection, normalizes locations to a consistent US address format when present in the source, and records detected language codes. Plagiarism checks run offline using submission fingerprinting and similarity thresholds (flag at 0.85 or higher) against prior local submissions and imported content; objective items auto-grade immediately, while subjective items route to a TA or Instructor queue with status transitions and mandatory rubric-based scoring, and any schedule change triggers an impact prompt plus undo/redo backed by a change journal, while locked periods reject conflicting edits with an explanatory error message.
